items.title: How to Save and Load TensorFlow Models
items.author: Tensor Flow - [Technical Documentation]
items.content:
• Saving from tf.keras training APIs
  • Manually inspecting checkpoints

\ \ The phrase "Saving a TensorFlow model" typically means one of two things:

    Checkpoints capture the exact value of all parameters ( objects) used by a model. Checkpoints do not contain any description of the computation defined by the model and thus are typically only useful when source code that will use the saved parameter values is available.

    The SavedModel format on the other hand includes a serialized description of the computation defined by the model in addition to the parameter values (checkpoint). Models in this format are independent of the source code that created the model. They are thus suitable for deployment via TensorFlow Serving, TensorFlow Lite, TensorFlow.js, or programs in other programming languages (the C, C++, Java, Go, Rust, C# etc. TensorFlow APIs).

    This guide covers APIs for writing and reading checkpoints.

    class Net(tf.keras.Model):
  """A simple linear model."""

  def __init__(self):
    super(Net, self).__init__()
    self.l1 = tf.keras.layers.Dense(5)

  def call(self, x):
    return self.l1(x)

    Saving from  training APIs

    See the  guide on saving and restoring.

    tf.keras.Model.save_weights saves a TensorFlow checkpoint.

    net.save_weights('easy_checkpoint')

    The persistent state of a TensorFlow model is stored in  objects. These can be constructed directly, but are often created through high-level APIs like  or .

    The easiest way to manage variables is by attaching them to Python objects, then referencing those objects.

    Subclasses of , , and  automatically track variables assigned to their attributes. The following example constructs a simple linear model, then writes checkpoints which contain values for all of the model's variables.

    You can easily save a model-checkpoint with .

    To help demonstrate all the features of , define a toy dataset and optimization step:

    def toy_dataset():
  inputs = tf.range(10.)[:, None]
  labels = inputs * 5. + tf.range(5.)[None, :]
  return tf.data.Dataset.from_tensor_slices(
    dict(x=inputs, y=labels)).repeat().batch(2)

    def train_step(net, example, optimizer):
  """Trains `net` on `example` using `optimizer`."""
  with tf.GradientTape() as tape:
    output = net(example['x'])
    loss = tf.reduce_mean(tf.abs(output - example['y']))
  variables = net.trainable_variables
  gradients = tape.gradient(loss, variables)
  optimizer.apply_gradients(zip(gradients, variables))
  return loss

    Create the checkpoint objects

    Use a  object to manually create a checkpoint, where the objects you want to checkpoint are set as attributes on the object.

    A tf.train.CheckpointManager can also be helpful for managing multiple checkpoints.

    opt = tf.keras.optimizers.Adam(0.1)
dataset = toy_dataset()
iterator = iter(dataset)
ckpt = tf.train.Checkpoint(step=tf.Variable(1), optimizer=opt, net=net, iterator=iterator)
manager = tf.train.CheckpointManager(ckpt, './tf_ckpts', max_to_keep=3)

    Train and checkpoint the model

    The following training loop creates an instance of the model and of an optimizer, then gathers them into a  object. It calls the training step in a loop on each batch of data, and periodically writes checkpoints to disk.

    def train_and_checkpoint(net, manager):
  ckpt.restore(manager.latest_checkpoint)
  if manager.latest_checkpoint:
    print("Restored from {}".format(manager.latest_checkpoint))
  else:
    print("Initializing from scratch.")

  for _ in range(50):
    example = next(iterator)
    loss = train_step(net, example, opt)
    ckpt.step.assign_add(1)
    if int(ckpt.step) % 10 == 0:
      save_path = manager.save()
      print("Saved checkpoint for step {}: {}".format(int(ckpt.step), save_path))
      print("loss {:1.2f}".format(loss.numpy()))

    train_and_checkpoint(net, manager)

    Restore and continue training

    After the first training cycle you can pass a new model and manager, but pick up training exactly where you left off:

    opt = tf.keras.optimizers.Adam(0.1)
net = Net()
dataset = toy_dataset()
iterator = iter(dataset)
ckpt = tf.train.Checkpoint(step=tf.Variable(1), optimizer=opt, net=net, iterator=iterator)
manager = tf.train.CheckpointManager(ckpt, './tf_ckpts', max_to_keep=3)

train_and_checkpoint(net, manager)

    The tf.train.CheckpointManager object deletes old checkpoints. Above it's configured to keep only the three most recent checkpoints.

    print(manager.checkpoints)  # List the three remaining checkpoints

    These paths, e.g. , are not files on disk. Instead they are prefixes for an  file and one or more data files which contain the variable values. These prefixes are grouped together in a single  file () where the  saves its state.

    TensorFlow matches variables to checkpointed values by traversing a directed graph with named edges, starting from the object being loaded. Edge names typically come from attribute names in objects, for example the  in self.l1 = tf.keras.layers.Dense(5).  uses its keyword argument names, as in the  in tf.train.Checkpoint(step=...).

    The dependency graph from the example above looks like this:

    The optimizer is in red, regular variables are in blue, and the optimizer slot variables are in orange. The other nodes—for example, representing the —are in black.

    Slot variables are part of the optimizer's state, but are created for a specific variable. For example, the  edges above correspond to momentum, which the Adam optimizer tracks for each variable. Slot variables are only saved in a checkpoint if the variable and the optimizer would both be saved, thus the dashed edges.

    Calling  on a  object queues the requested restorations, restoring variable values as soon as there's a matching path from the  object. For example, you can load just the bias from the model you defined above by reconstructing one path to it through the network and the layer.

    to_restore = tf.Variable(tf.zeros([5]))
print(to_restore.numpy())  # All zeros
fake_layer = tf.train.Checkpoint(bias=to_restore)
fake_net = tf.train.Checkpoint(l1=fake_layer)
new_root = tf.train.Checkpoint(net=fake_net)
status = new_root.restore(tf.train.latest_checkpoint('./tf_ckpts/'))
print(to_restore.numpy())  # This gets the restored value.

    The dependency graph for these new objects is a much smaller subgraph of the larger checkpoint you wrote above. It includes only the bias and a save counter that  uses to number checkpoints.

     returns a status object, which has optional assertions. All of the objects created in the new  have been restored, so status.assert_existing_objects_matched passes.

    status.assert_existing_objects_matched()

    There are many objects in the checkpoint which haven't matched, including the layer's kernel and the optimizer's variables.  only passes if the checkpoint and the program match exactly, and would throw an exception here.

     objects in TensorFlow may defer the creation of variables to their first call, when input shapes are available. For example, the shape of a  layer's kernel depends on both the layer's input and output shapes, and so the output shape required as a constructor argument is not enough information to create the variable on its own. Since calling a  also reads the variable's value, a restore must happen between the variable's creation and its first use.

    To support this idiom,  defers restores which don't yet have a matching variable.

    deferred_restore = tf.Variable(tf.zeros([1, 5]))
print(deferred_restore.numpy())  # Not restored; still zeros
fake_layer.kernel = deferred_restore
print(deferred_restore.numpy())  # Restored

    Manually inspecting checkpoints

     returns a  that gives lower level access to the checkpoint contents. It contains mappings from each variable's key, to the shape and dtype for each variable in the checkpoint. A variable's key is its object path, like in the graphs displayed above.

    :::tip  There is no higher level structure to the checkpoint. It only know's the paths and values for the variables, and has no concept of ,  or how they are connected.

    reader = tf.train.load_checkpoint('./tf_ckpts/')
shape_from_key = reader.get_variable_to_shape_map()
dtype_from_key = reader.get_variable_to_dtype_map()

sorted(shape_from_key.keys())

    So if you're interested in the value of  you can get the value with the following code:

    key = 'net/l1/kernel/.ATTRIBUTES/VARIABLE_VALUE'

print("Shape:", shape_from_key[key])
print("Dtype:", dtype_from_key[key].name)

    It also provides a  method allowing you to inspect the value of a variable:

    Checkpoints save and restore the values of  objects by "tracking" any variable or trackable object set in one of its attributes. When executing a save, variables are gathered recursively from all of the reachable tracked objects.

    As with direct attribute assignments like self.l1 = tf.keras.layers.Dense(5), assigning lists and dictionaries to attributes will track their contents.

    save = tf.train.Checkpoint()
save.listed = [tf.Variable(1.)]
save.listed.append(tf.Variable(2.))
save.mapped = {'one': save.listed[0]}
save.mapped['two'] = save.listed[1]
save_path = save.save('./tf_list_example')

restore = tf.train.Checkpoint()
v2 = tf.Variable(0.)
assert 0. == v2.numpy()  # Not restored yet
restore.mapped = {'two': v2}
restore.restore(save_path)
assert 2. == v2.numpy()

    You may notice wrapper objects for lists and dictionaries. These wrappers are checkpointable versions of the underlying data-structures. Just like the attribute based loading, these wrappers restore a variable's value as soon as it's added to the container.

    restore.listed = []
print(restore.listed)  # ListWrapper([])
v1 = tf.Variable(0.)
restore.listed.append(v1)  # Restores v1, from restore() in the previous cell
assert 1. == v1.numpy()

    Trackable objects include ,  and its subclasses (e.g.  and ), and recognized Python containers:

    •  (and )
    •  (and , )

    Other container types are , including:

      All other Python objects are , including:

      TensorFlow objects provide an easy automatic mechanism for saving and restoring the values of variables they use.

      :::info Originally published on the  website, this article appears here under a new headline and is licensed under CC BY 4.0. Code samples shared under the Apache 2.0 License.

items.title: Physicists Blow Up Gold With Giant Lasers, Accidentally Disprove Renowned Physics Model
items.author: BeauHD
items.content:
Physicists at SLAC National Accelerator Laboratory superheated gold to over 33,000F using giant lasers and X-rays -- far exceeding the limits set by long-standing physics models. From the report: In an experiment presented today in Nature, researchers, for the first time ever, demonstrated a way to directly measure the temperature of matter in extreme states, or conditions with intensely high temperatures, pressures, or densities. Using the new technique, scientists succeeded in capturing gold at a temperature far beyond its boiling point -- a procedure called superheating -- at which point the common metal existed in a strange limbo between solid and liquid. The results suggest that, under the right conditions, gold may have no superheating limit. If true, this could have a wide range of applications across spaceflight, astrophysics, or nuclear chemistry, according to the researchers.
 
The study is based on a two-pronged experiment. First, the scientists used a laser to superheat a sample of gold, suppressing the metal's natural tendency to expand when heated. Next, they used ultrabright X-rays to zap the gold samples, which scattered off the surface of the gold. By calculating the distortions in the X-ray's frequency after colliding with the gold particles, the team locked down the speed and temperature of the atoms.
 
The experimental result seemingly refutes a well-established theory in physics, which states that structures like gold can't be heated more than three times their boiling point, 1,948 degrees Fahrenheit (1,064 degrees Celsius). Beyond those temperatures, superheated gold is supposed to reach the so-called "entropy catastrophe" -- or, in more colloquial terms, the heated gold should've blown up. The researchers themselves didn't expect to surpass that limit. The new result disproves the conventional theory, but it does so in a big way by far overshooting the theoretical prediction, showing that it's possible to heat gold up to a jaw-dropping 33,740 degrees F (18,726 degrees C). [...] The team is already applying the technique to other materials, such as silver and iron, which they happily report produced some promising data.

items.title: Will Your Next Autonomous Vehicle Drive You Into a Cyberattack?
items.author: Sandeep Dommari
items.content:
Autonomous vehicles (AVs) are a rapidly developing reality that has the potential to completely transform global transportation systems; they are no longer just a sci-fi idea. These self-driving vehicles, which are being tested and put on the road thanks to advanced technologies like artificial intelligence (AI), machine learning, sensor fusion, and real-time data processing, have the potential to transform how we commute, move cargo, and engage with our surroundings. Improved mobility for people with disabilities, decreased emissions, less traffic, and increased safety are all potential benefits of autonomous vehicles. However, AVs also bring with them new and serious cybersecurity challenges as they are incorporated more and more into the transportation ecosystem. These systems are vulnerable to cyberattacks that could have disastrous outcomes because of their intricacy, dependence on cutting-edge technologies, and volume of data transferred between the vehicle and external systems. Because opportunities and risks must be carefully balanced to ensure that autonomous vehicles (AVs) operate safely and securely, the intersection of cybersecurity and AV technology thus becomes a pressing area of focus.

\ A variety of sensors, such as lidar, radar, ultrasonic sensors, and cameras, are installed in autonomous cars to give them situational awareness and enable them to recognize and react to their surroundings. The car can make decisions like navigating roads, avoiding obstacles, and modifying speed thanks to the data these sensors provide to sophisticated machine learning algorithms. Furthermore, AVs are able to communicate with other vehicles and roadside infrastructure through vehicle-to-vehicle (V2V) and vehicle-to-infrastructure (V2I) communication technologies, exchanging critical data regarding traffic conditions, accidents, and road hazards. Although these features are supposed to increase driving efficiency and safety, they also create a lot of vulnerabilities that hackers could take advantage of. Because AVs are linked and dependent on external data, they are more susceptible to hacker attacks. They could exploit vulnerabilities in the hardware, software, or communication systems of the vehicle to gain control, steal personal data, or cause issues. Unauthorized access and data breaches are not the only risks related to cybersecurity in autonomous vehicles. An AV hack could have disastrous effects, including altering vehicle behavior and resulting in collisions, injuries, or even death. Critical systems like the vehicle's steering, braking, or navigation could be taken over by attackers, impairing the AV's capacity to make safe decisions. Additionally, because AVs are depending more and more on cloud computing for data processing and storage, they are vulnerable to cyberattacks that target their cloud infrastructure, which stores enormous volumes of data ranging from passenger information to driving patterns. Since passenger personal information, such as travel destinations, preferences, and behavioral data, may be accessed and misused, data privacy and user authentication also pose serious risks. Cybersecurity is now a crucial component of autonomous vehicle design and operation rather than an optional extra. Cybercriminals might take advantage of software flaws that antivirus software automatically uses to make decisions. Over-the-Air (OTA) updates are used by vehicles to improve functionality and address bugs, but they also create new avenues for attack. The performance and integrity of the car's system could be jeopardized if malicious actors took advantage of flaws in the OTA update procedure to introduce malware. As a result, cybersecurity needs to be a key component of the AV development cycle, involving patch management, frequent vulnerability assessments, and secure coding techniques to fix any vulnerabilities before they can be exploited. The entire AV ecosystem, which consists of interactions between vehicles, cloud infrastructure, and external communication networks, is more complicated to secure than just individual vehicles. New security challenges are brought about by the vehicle-to-everything (V2X) communication paradigm, which makes it easier for AVs to interact with their surroundings. To stop illegal devices from mimicking other automobiles or infrastructure elements, secure communication protocols must be in place. Preventing cyberattacks that manipulate data, like replay or spoofing attacks, requires AVs and other entities to ensure the authenticity and integrity of the data they exchange.

Developing strong cybersecurity frameworks is crucial because the vulnerability to cyberattacks will increase as the number of AVs on the road rises. Adopting industry best practices and standards is essential to AV system security. Some of the most important cybersecurity requirements for AVs are starting to be addressed by standards like those from the Society of Automotive Engineers (SAE) and the International Organization for Standardization (ISO). But there are still a lot of gaps in these frameworks, which are still in their infancy. Cybersecurity standards and regulations for autonomous vehicles are being developed by the National Highway Traffic Safety Administration (NHTSA) in the United States and other regulatory agencies worldwide; however, there is still work to be done to harmonize these standards internationally. Furthermore, establishing a unified and safe environment for AV deployment requires cooperation between the government, cybersecurity specialists, and the automotive industry. Maintaining the resilience of AV systems will require ongoing monitoring, threat intelligence sharing, and incident response tactics as cybersecurity threats continue to change. To guarantee that cybersecurity is given top priority throughout the entire autonomous vehicle development lifecycle, from concept to deployment and beyond, organizations must fund employee awareness and training initiatives.

1. Overview of Autonomous Vehicle Technology

Self-driving technology, sometimes known as autonomous vehicle (AV) technology, has advanced significantly in recent years and has the potential to completely change how people travel in the future. Fundamentally, autonomous cars can sense their surroundings, make choices, and drive themselves without assistance from a human. Numerous cutting-edge technologies, such as machine learning, artificial intelligence (AI), sensors, data processing, and communication networks, are integrated to accomplish this amazing feat. The ultimate goal of autonomous vehicles is to develop a smart, safe, and efficient transportation system that can minimize human error, avoid collisions, maximize traffic flow, and increase mobility—especially for people who might have trouble driving, like the elderly or people with disabilities. A variety of sensors that offer real-time information about the vehicle's environment are crucial to autonomous cars. Together, these sensors—which include lidar (Light Detection and Ranging), radar, ultrasonic sensors, and cameras—provide a thorough picture of the surroundings. For example, Lidar makes high-resolution 3D maps of the environment and measures distances using laser beams. By emitting radio waves and sound pulses, respectively, radar and ultrasonic sensors assist in detecting objects like other cars, pedestrians, and obstacles. By providing visual information, cameras help the car recognize and understand traffic signals, road signs, pedestrians, and other important environmental factors. Driving decisions are then made using the 360-degree view of the vehicle's surroundings that is produced by fusing the data from these sensors. Algorithms for machine learning and artificial intelligence power an autonomous car's decision-making. These algorithms use the information from the sensors to interpret the environment around the car, anticipate possible dangers, and decide how to accelerate, brake, steer, and change lanes. The vehicle's system is trained using machine learning, especially deep learning, to identify patterns in the surroundings and gradually enhance its decision-making. These AI systems allow AVs to accomplish complicated tasks like navigating intersections, passing other cars, and reacting to abrupt changes in traffic conditions by learning from large datasets and real-world driving scenarios. The ability of autonomous vehicles to communicate with infrastructure, cloud-based systems, and other vehicles is essential to their operation. Roadside units, traffic management systems, and other vehicles on the road can all receive information from AVs thanks to Vehicle-to-Vehicle (V2V) and Vehicle-to-Infrastructure (V2I) communication technologies. Real-time information about traffic patterns, road closures, collisions, and other important variables that influence driving decisions can be obtained through this communication. By allowing AVs to communicate with a variety of objects, including smart traffic lights, pedestrians' mobile devices, and even city infrastructure, Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) communication also improves the vehicle's awareness and decision-making abilities. According to the degree of automation, autonomous car technology is usually divided into five levels, ranging from Level 0 (no automation) to Level 5 (complete automation). Vehicles currently come equipped with Level 1 and Level 2 systems, which entail partial automation. These consist of functions like lane-keeping assistance and adaptive cruise control. Higher automation levels, like Level 4 and Level 5, allow AVs to drive entirely on their own with little to no assistance from humans. While Level 5 vehicles are completely autonomous in all circumstances and do not require a human driver, Level 4 vehicles can only operate autonomously in certain situations, such as within predetermined geographic boundaries or on predetermined routes. Even though autonomous car technology is developing quickly, there are still a number of obstacles to overcome, such as issues with data processing, real-time decision-making, and sensor reliability. Before widespread adoption, ethical and regulatory concerns must also be addressed, including privacy issues, liability in the event of an accident, and the requirement for international standards. However, the continued advancement of autonomous car technology has the potential to completely transform transportation by providing notable gains in accessibility, efficiency, and safety.

2. Role of Artificial Intelligence in Autonomous Driving

The development and operation of autonomous vehicles (AVs) are greatly aided by artificial intelligence (AI), which allows them to carry out intricate tasks that would typically require human drivers. The core of autonomous driving is the vehicle's capacity to sense its environment, make decisions in real time, and respond to those decisions without the need for human intervention. The incorporation of AI-driven technologies enables AVs to process enormous volumes of sensory data, gain experience, and gradually enhance their decision-making abilities, thereby achieving such autonomy. In addition to being the foundation of a vehicle's operational intelligence, artificial intelligence (AI) is also in charge of continuously improving safety, effectiveness, and adaptability in changing conditions. Machine learning (ML), a branch of AI that enables AV systems to learn from data and experience, is at the core of AI in autonomous driving. To identify and interpret the vehicle's surroundings, machine learning algorithms examine data produced by the sensors, which include lidar, radar, ultrasonic sensors, and cameras. Real-time data about pedestrians, other cars, road conditions, traffic signals, obstacles, and other environmental elements is provided by these sensors. AVs can make crucial driving decisions like when to stop, accelerate, or change lanes thanks to machine learning models, especially those that use deep learning techniques. By analyzing new data and modifying its behavior appropriately, the system gradually gains proficiency in managing progressively complex driving scenarios.

\ The vehicle can "see" and comprehend its surroundings similarly to how humans do thanks to computer vision, another essential AI technique. Computer vision algorithms can identify and detect objects, signs, road markings, and other vehicles using data from cameras and sensors, guaranteeing safe navigation in real time. AVs can respond to a variety of potential road hazards thanks to AI-powered computer vision that enables them to recognize cyclists, pedestrians, and even animals. AVs can also recognize traffic signs and signals thanks to this technology, which guarantees that the car complies with traffic laws and drives safely in a variety of situations. Autonomous cars can make wise driving decisions thanks to AI-powered decision-making algorithms. To choose the best course of action in intricate traffic situations, these algorithms integrate data from multiple sensors and sources. For example, the AV's AI system makes decisions about speed and positioning in real time when navigating through a busy intersection by analyzing a number of variables, including the direction and speed of other vehicles, pedestrians' proximity, and traffic light signals. AI's role in autonomous driving is characterized by its capacity to evaluate these variables and make snap decisions. AI also makes it possible for AVs to communicate with other vehicles (V2V) and infrastructure (V2I), which improves their situational awareness and decision-making. Through V2V communication, AVs can share information with other cars on the road, preventing collisions, improving traffic flow, and enabling them to make defensible decisions based on data from other cars in the vicinity. In order to optimize routes, predict changes in traffic conditions, and guarantee smoother travel, V2I communication enables the vehicle to communicate with smart traffic infrastructure, such as traffic lights and sensors. This extra data is processed and interpreted by AI to improve the vehicle's situational awareness. The potential of AI to increase safety is among its most significant contributions to autonomous driving. AI-enabled autonomous cars are far quicker and more accurate than human drivers at analyzing sensor data and road conditions. In many circumstances, artificial intelligence (AI) systems are more dependable than human drivers because they are not distracted or fatigued and are able to continuously monitor their surroundings. AVs can lower the risk of accidents caused by human error, which is a major cause of traffic collisions, by combining AI-driven perception, decision-making, and control. Even with these developments, there are still issues with AI in autonomous driving. These include treating edge cases, in which the AI system comes across circumstances for which it has not been trained, guaranteeing robustness in a variety of unpredictable driving environments, and resolving ethical and decision-making issues in dire circumstances. Furthermore, high-quality data and ongoing learning from actual driving experiences are crucial for AI system safety because they guarantee that the car can adjust to novel and changing situations.

3. The Interconnected Ecosystem: Vehicles, Infrastructure, and Cloud

Autonomous vehicles (AVs) have developed into a more complex ecosystem that encompasses not only the vehicle but also cloud-based systems and the surrounding infrastructure. Because it enables real-time communication, data sharing, and coordination between multiple components—all crucial for guaranteeing safety, effectiveness, and adaptability in complex environments—this interconnected ecosystem is crucial to the success of autonomous driving. AVs can operate at their best, react to traffic conditions in real time, and make wise decisions while driving thanks to the smooth integration of infrastructure, cloud services, and automobiles. To fully grasp autonomous driving technology and its potential to revolutionize transportation, it is essential to comprehend how these interrelated components relate to one another. The vehicle, which is at the center of this ecosystem, uses a variety of sensors, cameras, and computer systems to sense and comprehend its surroundings. Technologies like lidar, radar, ultrasonic sensors, and cameras are installed in autonomous cars to gather information about their environment, including other cars, pedestrians, obstructions, and traffic patterns. The onboard systems of the car process the data from these sensors using machine learning and artificial intelligence (AI) algorithms to allow the car to make decisions in real time. The vehicle's capabilities, however, extend beyond its onboard systems; it is a component of a broader ecosystem in which communication with cloud and external infrastructure greatly improves its performance and decision-making. In this networked system, the infrastructure is essential.Autonomous vehicles can communicate with road infrastructure, including traffic signals, signs, road sensors, and smart city components, thanks to vehicle-to-infrastructure (V2I) communication. AVs can obtain vital information about traffic flow, road conditions, accidents, construction zones, and signal timings by establishing a connection with infrastructure. AVs can use this information to better plan their routes, steer clear of crowded areas, and obey traffic laws. For example, the AV can get real-time information about a broken traffic signal, allowing it to modify its behavior appropriately. Similar to this, autonomous vehicles (AVs) can coordinate their movements with intelligent traffic lights, which minimizes delays and maximizes fuel efficiency, particularly in urban areas. By interacting with infrastructure, AVs become more cognizant of their surroundings, which promotes safer and more effective driving. The cloud serves as a central location for data processing, analysis, and storage, adding another layer to this ecosystem. Autonomous vehicles can now access and share data with a wider network, including fleet management services, central traffic management systems, and other cars on the road, thanks to the cloud.Cloud-based services enable vehicle-to-vehicle (V2V) communication, which helps to improve traffic flow and prevent collisions by enabling AVs to share information about their positions, speeds, and planned actions. In order to keep autonomous cars safe and up to date, the cloud also facilitates over-the-air (OTA) updates, which enable software upgrades that include bug fixes, new features, and performance enhancements. Additionally, sophisticated algorithms and machine learning models that demand a lot of processing power can be supported by cloud computing resources, allowing AVs to continuously learn and adapt as they traverse different environments.

Additionally, the cloud offers a platform for data analytics, which enables the aggregation, analysis, and application of data gathered from infrastructure, environmental factors, and vehicles to enhance traffic control, vehicle performance, and safety procedures.Cities and municipalities can use big data analytics to plan infrastructure upgrades, optimize traffic patterns, and even forecast the need for vehicle and road maintenance. This data can be utilized by the automotive industry to improve system reliability, advance autonomous driving algorithms, and advance AV development. For autonomous driving to be successful, infrastructure, cloud systems, and automobiles must be integrated. In addition to improving situational awareness, safety, and efficiency, it facilitates real-time communication and data exchange. But there are also new difficulties brought about by this interconnected ecosystem, mainly with regard to interoperability, cybersecurity, and data privacy. Strong security measures are necessary to preserve the integrity and safety of the entire system since the attack surface for cyber threats grows as more automobiles, infrastructure parts, and cloud systems are connected. To guarantee that all parts can interact and function as a unit, it will also be essential to standardize communication protocols and systems among various manufacturers, cities, and nations.

4. Key Challenges in Securing Autonomous Vehicles

The swift advancement of autonomous vehicles (AVs) has the potential to revolutionize transportation in the future by providing enhanced efficiency, decreased traffic, and safety. These vehicles are susceptible to a number of security issues that must be resolved to guarantee their safe and secure operation, though, because they mainly rely on cutting-edge technologies like artificial intelligence (AI), machine learning, and sensor-based systems. Because they involve not just the vehicle but also an interconnected ecosystem of sensors, communication networks, cloud systems, and infrastructure, securing autonomous vehicles poses special and complicated challenges. From safeguarding the vehicle's internal systems to making sure that its external communications and interactions with other vehicles and infrastructure are secure, these challenges cover a broad spectrum of issues. Cybersecurity threats that target the vehicle's control systems and in-vehicle networks are one of the main obstacles to securing autonomous vehicles. Numerous sensors, including lidar, radar, cameras, and ultrasonic sensors, are installed in autonomous cars to provide vital information for safe driving. The electronic control units (ECUs) of the car process the data from these sensors and make decisions in real time. These networks are vulnerable to malware, hacking, and data manipulation, among other cyberattacks. An attacker could potentially compromise vital functions like braking, steering, or acceleration by manipulating the vehicle's control systems if they manage to access the internal network of the vehicle. This could have disastrous results. Preventing unwanted access to vital control systems and safeguarding the in-vehicle network from outside threats are of utmost importance. Another major security issue for AVs is remote access vulnerabilities. Vehicle-to-vehicle (V2V) and vehicle-to-infrastructure (V2I) communications are two of the many uses for wireless communication systems in contemporary automobiles. These systems create new attack avenues even as they improve the vehicle's interaction with its surroundings and other vehicles. Hackers may use wireless communication channels to spoof communication signals, introduce malicious software, or obtain unauthorized access to the car. An attacker might pose as a nearby car or piece of infrastructure, for instance, to trick the AV into making risky driving choices. The vehicle must be protected from such attacks by securing communication protocols, making sure encryption is correct, and authenticating external signals. Another crucial issue in protecting autonomous cars is passenger data privacy. It is crucial to make sure that the enormous volumes of personal data that AVs gather—such as location data, travel patterns, and occasionally even health data—are adequately protected and not misused. A large portion of this data is processed and stored in the cloud, which is connected to autonomous cars. They are therefore susceptible to illegal data collection and data breaches. To safeguard passengers' privacy and stop data from being misused, it is crucial to implement secure data storage, data anonymization, and privacy-preserving algorithms. AV security is further complicated by over-the-air (OTA) updates. Autonomous vehicles need software updates to enhance functionality, address bugs, and patch security flaws, just like conventional cars. OTA updates, however, also offer a possible point of attack. An attacker could introduce malicious code into the car's system and jeopardize its security or cause malfunctions if they manage to take over the update process. Mitigating this risk requires implementing secure boot mechanisms, ensuring the integrity of the OTA update process, and using digital signatures to confirm the legitimacy of updates. Finally, securing autonomous vehicles also presents significant regulatory and compliance challenges. The security of these vehicles is not governed by widely recognized standards and laws because the AV industry is still developing. The regulatory frameworks of various nations and regions differ, and the lack of international agreement on cybersecurity standards for antivirus software makes it more difficult to create secure systems. Furthermore, regulators find it challenging to keep up with technological advancements due to the complexity of AV systems. Manufacturers may apply security measures inconsistently as a result of unclear, unenforced security standards, which could leave holes for hackers to take advantage of. To sum up, protecting autonomous cars is a complex issue that calls for an all-encompassing strategy to handle cybersecurity risks, safeguard data privacy, establish secure communication channels, and guarantee the integrity of software updates. Strong security measures, international standards, and efficient regulatory frameworks will be more and more important as technology develops. Ensuring their safety and security is essential to the autonomous vehicle industry's success and the public's faith in this game-changing technology.

5. Importance of Cybersecurity in Ensuring Safe AV Deployment

With the potential to enhance safety, boost traffic efficiency, and lessen environmental impact, the introduction of autonomous vehicles (AVs) marks a substantial advancement in transportation technology. To guarantee that AVs can function safely in real-world settings, a number of security issues that come with these developments must be resolved. Cybersecurity is essential for protecting autonomous vehicles from a variety of possible risks, such as system hacks and data breaches, so that AVs can operate without endangering drivers, passengers, or pedestrians. Without robust cybersecurity defenses, AVs are exposed to a range of cyberattacks that could jeopardize vehicle functionality and have disastrous results. The protection of vehicle control systems is one of the main reasons cybersecurity is crucial for AV deployment. Electronic control units (ECUs), which regulate vital operations like braking, steering, and acceleration, are essential to autonomous cars. In order to facilitate safe and effective navigation, these systems are highly integrated and rely on sensors such as lidar, radar, and cameras. Cybercriminals could take over the car, alter its behavior, and jeopardize its safety if they manage to access these control systems. To put the lives of people inside the car and other drivers in danger, a hacker might, for instance, disable the braking system or cause the car to accelerate uncontrollably. Making AVs safe for deployment requires that these control systems be protected from outside cyber threats.With the potential to enhance safety, boost traffic efficiency, and lessen environmental impact, the introduction of autonomous vehicles (AVs) marks a substantial advancement in transportation technology. To guarantee that AVs can function safely in real-world settings, a number of security issues that come with these developments must be resolved. Cybersecurity is essential for protecting autonomous vehicles from a variety of possible risks, such as system hacks and data breaches, so that AVs can operate without endangering drivers, passengers, or pedestrians. Without robust cybersecurity defenses, AVs are exposed to a range of cyberattacks that could jeopardize vehicle functionality and have disastrous results. The protection of vehicle control systems is one of the main reasons cybersecurity is crucial for AV deployment. Electronic control units (ECUs), which regulate vital operations like braking, steering, and acceleration, are essential to autonomous cars. In order to facilitate safe and effective navigation, these systems are highly integrated and rely on sensors such as lidar, radar, and cameras. Cybercriminals could take over the car, alter its behavior, and jeopardize its safety if they manage to access these control systems. To put the lives of people inside the car and other drivers in danger, a hacker might, for instance, disable the braking system or cause the car to accelerate uncontrollably. Making AVs safe for deployment requires that these control systems be protected from outside cyber threats.

There are particular and pressing issues at the nexus of cybersecurity and driverless cars that call for an all-encompassing and proactive strategy. Cybersecurity needs to be integrated into the very foundation of AV development and operation as it advances. Strong cybersecurity measures will be necessary to guarantee that autonomous vehicles are safe, secure, and reliable, from protecting in-vehicle systems and communication networks to handling privacy issues and outside threats. Realizing the full potential of autonomous vehicles while reducing the risks associated with this game-changing technology will require tackling these cybersecurity issues head-on. Understanding the fundamental cybersecurity problems that autonomous vehicles (AVs) encounter is made easier by this chapter, which also establishes the framework for the chapters that follow, in which we will delve deeply into the tactics, tools, and best practices required to secure AVs. In order to achieve completely automated transportation, autonomous vehicle technology combines several state-of-the-art technologies. Self-driving cars are becoming a real and exciting possibility as a result of the integration of sensors, machine learning, artificial intelligence, and communication networks, which is changing how cars see and interact with their surroundings. Even though there are still obstacles to overcome, ongoing development and testing of autonomous car systems is opening the door to a time when driving itself will be normalized, drastically changing the way we travel. The foundation of autonomous driving technology is artificial intelligence (AI), which gives cars the perception, decision-making, and autonomous behavior they need. AI gives AVs the ability to navigate dynamic and complex environments with high levels of safety and efficiency by combining machine learning, computer vision, decision-making algorithms, and communication systems. AI technology will play an increasingly important role in autonomous driving as it develops further, propelling transportation toward a new era of completely intelligent and autonomous vehicles. The core of autonomous driving technology is the networked ecosystem of cloud computing, infrastructure, and driverless cars. Vehicles can navigate more safely, adjust to changing conditions, and communicate with nearby infrastructure more intelligently thanks to this ecosystem. The future of transportation depends on the integration of these elements, which provide a more effective, sustainable, and secure driving experience by facilitating continuous data flow, real-time communication, and advanced analytics. To fully realize this ecosystem's potential, however, it will be imperative to address the issues of cybersecurity, privacy, and standardization as it grows. An essential component of the safe implementation of autonomous vehicles is cybersecurity. Malicious attacks that could jeopardize vehicle safety, interfere with operations, and erode public trust in AV technology must be avoided by protecting vital vehicle systems, securing communication channels, protecting data privacy, and protecting the software update process. Strong cybersecurity measures will be even more important as autonomous vehicles develop and become more incorporated into the transportation ecosystem. This will guarantee that AVs can fulfill their promises of convenience, safety, and efficiency without putting people or society at unnecessary risk.

items.title: Randomness As Defined by Machine Learning’s Most Popular Language
items.author: Tensor Flow - [Technical Documentation]
items.content:
• The tf.random.Generator class
  • Creating independent random-number streams
  • Interaction with tf.function
  • Interaction with distribution strategies

\ TensorFlow provides a set of pseudo-random number generators (RNG), in the  module. This document describes how you can control the random number generators, and how these generators interact with other tensorflow sub-systems.

:::tip  The random numbers are not guaranteed to be consistent across TensorFlow versions. See: 

TensorFlow provides two approaches for controlling the random number generation process:

  1. Through the explicit use of  objects. Each such object maintains a state (in ) that will be changed after each number generation.
  2. Through the purely-functional stateless random functions like tf.random.stateless_uniform. Calling these functions with the same arguments (which include the seed) and on the same device will always produce the same results.

:::warning  The old RNGs from TF 1.x such as  and  are not yet deprecated but strongly discouraged.

import tensorflow as tf

# Creates some virtual devices (cpu:0, cpu:1, etc.) for using distribution strategy
physical_devices = tf.config.list_physical_devices("CPU")
tf.config.experimental.set_virtual_device_configuration(
    physical_devices[0], [
        tf.config.experimental.VirtualDeviceConfiguration(),
        tf.config.experimental.VirtualDeviceConfiguration(),
        tf.config.experimental.VirtualDeviceConfiguration()
    ])

See more at https://github.com/torvalds/linux/blob/v6.0/Documentation/ABI/testing/sysfs-bus-pci#L344-L355

The  class

The  class is used in cases where you want each RNG call to produce different results. It maintains an internal state (managed by a  object) which will be updated every time random numbers are generated. Because the state is managed by , it enjoys all facilities provided by  such as easy checkpointing, automatic control-dependency and thread safety.

You can get a  by manually creating an object of the class or call tf.random.get_global_generator() to get the default global generator:

g1 = tf.random.Generator.from_seed(1)
print(g1.normal(shape=[2, 3]))
g2 = tf.random.get_global_generator()
print(g2.normal(shape=[2, 3]))

See more at https://github.com/torvalds/linux/blob/v6.0/Documentation/ABI/testing/sysfs-bus-pci#L344-L355

There are multiple ways to create a generator object. The easiest is , as shown above, that creates a generator from a seed. A seed is any non-negative integer.  also takes an optional argument  which is the RNG algorithm that will be used by this generator:

g1 = tf.random.Generator.from_seed(1, alg='philox')
print(g1.normal(shape=[2, 3]))

tf.Tensor(
[[ 0.43842277 -0.53439844 -0.07710262]
 [ 1.5658045  -0.1012345  -0.2744976 ]], shape=(2, 3), dtype=float32)

See the  section below for more information about it.

Another way to create a generator is with Generator.from_non_deterministic_state. A generator created this way will start from a non-deterministic state, depending on e.g., time and OS.

g = tf.random.Generator.from_non_deterministic_state()
print(g.normal(shape=[2, 3]))

tf.Tensor(
[[ 0.9104948  -0.23143363 -0.09841432]
 [-0.91448975  0.1579936   1.3923475 ]], shape=(2, 3), dtype=float32)

There are yet other ways to create generators, such as from explicit states, which are not covered by this guide.

When using tf.random.get_global_generator to get the global generator, you need to be careful about device placement. The global generator is created (from a non-deterministic state) at the first time tf.random.get_global_generator is called, and placed on the default device at that call. So, for example, if the first site you call tf.random.get_global_generator is within a  scope, the global generator will be placed on the GPU, and using the global generator later on from the CPU will incur a GPU-to-CPU copy.

There is also a function tf.random.set_global_generator for replacing the global generator with another generator object. This function should be used with caution though, because the old global generator may have been captured by a  (as a weak reference), and replacing it will cause it to be garbage collected, breaking the . A better way to reset the global generator is to use one of the "reset" functions such as Generator.reset_from_seed, which won't create new generator objects.

g = tf.random.Generator.from_seed(1)
print(g.normal([]))
print(g.normal([]))
g.reset_from_seed(1)
print(g.normal([]))

tf.Tensor(0.43842277, shape=(), dtype=float32)
tf.Tensor(1.6272374, shape=(), dtype=float32)
tf.Tensor(0.43842277, shape=(), dtype=float32)

Creating independent random-number streams

In many applications one needs multiple independent random-number streams, independent in the sense that they won't overlap and won't have any statistically detectable correlations. This is achieved by using  to create multiple generators that are guaranteed to be independent of each other (i.e. generating independent streams).

g = tf.random.Generator.from_seed(1)
print(g.normal([]))
new_gs = g.split(3)
for new_g in new_gs:
  print(new_g.normal([]))
print(g.normal([]))

tf.Tensor(0.43842277, shape=(), dtype=float32)
tf.Tensor(2.536413, shape=(), dtype=float32)
tf.Tensor(0.33186463, shape=(), dtype=float32)
tf.Tensor(-0.07144657, shape=(), dtype=float32)
tf.Tensor(-0.79253083, shape=(), dtype=float32)

 will change the state of the generator on which it is called ( in the above example), similar to an RNG method such as . In addition to being independent of each other, the new generators () are also guaranteed to be independent of the old one ().

Spawning new generators is also useful when you want to make sure the generator you use is on the same device as other computations, to avoid the overhead of cross-device copy. For example:

with tf.device("cpu"):  # change "cpu" to the device you want
  g = tf.random.get_global_generator().split(1)[0]  
  print(g.normal([]))  # use of g won't cause cross-device copy, unlike the global generator

tf.Tensor(-0.66787744, shape=(), dtype=float32)

:::tip  In theory, you can use constructors such as  instead of  here to obtain a new generator, but by doing so you lose the guarantee that the new generator is independent of the global generator. You will also run the risk that you may accidentally create two generators with the same seed or with seeds that lead to overlapping random-number streams.

You can do splitting recursively, calling  on split generators. There are no limits (barring integer overflow) on the depth of recursions.

Interaction with 

 obeys the same rules as  when used with . This includes three aspects.

Creating generators outside 

 can use a generator created outside of it.

g = tf.random.Generator.from_seed(1)
@tf.function
def foo():
  return g.normal([])
print(foo())

tf.Tensor(0.43842277, shape=(), dtype=float32)

The user needs to make sure that the generator object is still alive (not garbage-collected) when the function is called.

Creating generators inside 

Creation of generators inside a  can only happened during the first run of the function.

g = None
@tf.function
def foo():
  global g
  if g is None:
    g = tf.random.Generator.from_seed(1)
  return g.normal([])
print(foo())
print(foo())

tf.Tensor(0.43842277, shape=(), dtype=float32)
tf.Tensor(1.6272374, shape=(), dtype=float32)

Passing generators as arguments to 

When used as an argument to a , different generator objects will cause retracing of the .

num_traces = 0
@tf.function
def foo(g):
  global num_traces
  num_traces += 1
  return g.normal([])
foo(tf.random.Generator.from_seed(1))
foo(tf.random.Generator.from_seed(2))
print(num_traces)

Note that this retracing behavior is consistent with :

num_traces = 0
@tf.function
def foo(v):
  global num_traces
  num_traces += 1
  return v.read_value()
foo(tf.Variable(1))
foo(tf.Variable(2))
print(num_traces)

Interaction with distribution strategies

There are two ways in which  interacts with distribution strategies.

Creating generators outside distribution strategies

If a generator is created outside strategy scopes, all replicas’ access to the generator will be serialized, and hence the replicas will get different random numbers.

g = tf.random.Generator.from_seed(1)
strat = tf.distribute.MirroredStrategy(devices=["cpu:0", "cpu:1"])
with strat.scope():
  def f():
    print(g.normal([]))
  results = strat.run(f)

INFO:tensorflow:Using MirroredStrategy with devices ('/job:localhost/replica:0/task:0/device:CPU:0', '/job:localhost/replica:0/task:0/device:CPU:1')
WARNING:tensorflow:Using MirroredStrategy eagerly has significant overhead currently. We will be working on improving this in the future, but for now please wrap `call_for_each_replica` or `experimental_run` or `run` inside a tf.function to get the best performance.
tf.Tensor(0.43842274, shape=(), dtype=float32)
tf.Tensor(1.6272374, shape=(), dtype=float32)

Note that this usage may have performance issues because the generator's device is different from the replicas.

Creating generators inside distribution strategies

If a generator is created inside a strategy scope, each replica will get a different and independent stream of random numbers.

strat = tf.distribute.MirroredStrategy(devices=["cpu:0", "cpu:1"])
with strat.scope():
  g = tf.random.Generator.from_seed(1)
  print(strat.run(lambda: g.normal([])))
  print(strat.run(lambda: g.normal([])))

INFO:tensorflow:Using MirroredStrategy with devices ('/job:localhost/replica:0/task:0/device:CPU:0', '/job:localhost/replica:0/task:0/device:CPU:1')

:::tip  Currently  doesn't provide an option to let different replicas get identical (instead of different) streams (which is technically not hard). If you have a use case for this feature, please let the TensorFlow developers know.

If the generator is seeded (e.g. created by ), the random numbers are determined by the seed, even though different replicas get different and uncorrelated numbers. One can think of a random number generated on a replica as a hash of the replica ID and a "primary" random number that is common to all replicas. Hence, the whole system is still deterministic.

 can also be created inside :

strat = tf.distribute.MirroredStrategy(devices=["cpu:0", "cpu:1"])
with strat.scope():
  def f():
    g = tf.random.Generator.from_seed(1)
    a = g.normal([])
    b = g.normal([])
    return tf.stack([a, b])
  print(strat.run(f))
  print(strat.run(f))

INFO:tensorflow:Using MirroredStrategy with devices ('/job:localhost/replica:0/task:0/device:CPU:0', '/job:localhost/replica:0/task:0/device:CPU:1')
WARNING:tensorflow:Using MirroredStrategy eagerly has significant overhead currently. We will be working on improving this in the future, but for now please wrap `call_for_each_replica` or `experimental_run` or `run` inside a tf.function to get the best performance.
PerReplica:{

  1: tf.Tensor([0.02066157 0.77539235], shape=(2,), dtype=float32)
}

  1: tf.Tensor([0.02066157 0.77539235], shape=(2,), dtype=float32)
}

We no longer recommend passing  as arguments to , because  generally expects the arguments to be tensors, not generators.

Generally for saving or serializing you can handle a  the same way you would handle a  or a  (or its subclasses). In TF there are two mechanisms for serialization: Checkpoint and SavedModel.

Generators can be freely saved and restored using . The random-number stream from the restoring point will be the same as that from the saving point.

filename = "./checkpoint"
g = tf.random.Generator.from_seed(1)
cp = tf.train.Checkpoint(generator=g)
print(g.normal([]))

tf.Tensor(0.43842277, shape=(), dtype=float32)

cp.write(filename)
print("RNG stream from saving point:")
print(g.normal([]))
print(g.normal([]))

RNG stream from saving point:
tf.Tensor(1.6272374, shape=(), dtype=float32)
tf.Tensor(1.6307176, shape=(), dtype=float32)

cp.restore(filename)
print("RNG stream from restoring point:")
print(g.normal([]))
print(g.normal([]))

RNG stream from restoring point:
tf.Tensor(1.6272374, shape=(), dtype=float32)
tf.Tensor(1.6307176, shape=(), dtype=float32)

You can also save and restore within a distribution strategy:

filename = "./checkpoint"
strat = tf.distribute.MirroredStrategy(devices=["cpu:0", "cpu:1"])
with strat.scope():
  g = tf.random.Generator.from_seed(1)
  cp = tf.train.Checkpoint(my_generator=g)
  print(strat.run(lambda: g.normal([])))

INFO:tensorflow:Using MirroredStrategy with devices ('/job:localhost/replica:0/task:0/device:CPU:0', '/job:localhost/replica:0/task:0/device:CPU:1')
INFO:tensorflow:Using MirroredStrategy with devices ('/job:localhost/replica:0/task:0/device:CPU:0', '/job:localhost/replica:0/task:0/device:CPU:1')
PerReplica:{
  0: tf.Tensor(-0.87930447, shape=(), dtype=float32),
  1: tf.Tensor(0.020661574, shape=(), dtype=float32)
}

with strat.scope():
  cp.write(filename)
  print("RNG stream from saving point:")
  print(strat.run(lambda: g.normal([])))
  print(strat.run(lambda: g.normal([])))

RNG stream from saving point:
PerReplica:{
  0: tf.Tensor(-1.5822568, shape=(), dtype=float32),
  1: tf.Tensor(0.77539235, shape=(), dtype=float32)
}
PerReplica:{
  0: tf.Tensor(-0.5039703, shape=(), dtype=float32),
  1: tf.Tensor(0.1251838, shape=(), dtype=float32)
}

with strat.scope():
  cp.restore(filename)
  print("RNG stream from restoring point:")
  print(strat.run(lambda: g.normal([])))
  print(strat.run(lambda: g.normal([])))

RNG stream from restoring point:
PerReplica:{
  0: tf.Tensor(-1.5822568, shape=(), dtype=float32),
  1: tf.Tensor(0.77539235, shape=(), dtype=float32)
}
PerReplica:{
  0: tf.Tensor(-0.5039703, shape=(), dtype=float32),
  1: tf.Tensor(0.1251838, shape=(), dtype=float32)
}

You should make sure that the replicas don't diverge in their RNG call history (e.g. one replica makes one RNG call while another makes two RNG calls) before saving. Otherwise, their internal RNG states will diverge and  (which only saves the first replica's state) won't properly restore all the replicas.

You can also restore a saved checkpoint to a different distribution strategy with a different number of replicas. Because a  object created in a strategy can only be used in the same strategy, to restore to a different strategy, you have to create a new  in the target strategy and a new  for it, as shown in this example:

filename = "./checkpoint"
strat1 = tf.distribute.MirroredStrategy(devices=["cpu:0", "cpu:1"])
with strat1.scope():
  g1 = tf.random.Generator.from_seed(1)
  cp1 = tf.train.Checkpoint(my_generator=g1)
  print(strat1.run(lambda: g1.normal([])))

INFO:tensorflow:Using MirroredStrategy with devices ('/job:localhost/replica:0/task:0/device:CPU:0', '/job:localhost/replica:0/task:0/device:CPU:1')
INFO:tensorflow:Using MirroredStrategy with devices ('/job:localhost/replica:0/task:0/device:CPU:0', '/job:localhost/replica:0/task:0/device:CPU:1')
PerReplica:{
  0: tf.Tensor(-0.87930447, shape=(), dtype=float32),
  1: tf.Tensor(0.020661574, shape=(), dtype=float32)
}

with strat1.scope():
  cp1.write(filename)
  print("RNG stream from saving point:")
  print(strat1.run(lambda: g1.normal([])))
  print(strat1.run(lambda: g1.normal([])))

RNG stream from saving point:
PerReplica:{
  0: tf.Tensor(-1.5822568, shape=(), dtype=float32),
  1: tf.Tensor(0.77539235, shape=(), dtype=float32)
}
PerReplica:{
  0: tf.Tensor(-0.5039703, shape=(), dtype=float32),
  1: tf.Tensor(0.1251838, shape=(), dtype=float32)
}

strat2 = tf.distribute.MirroredStrategy(devices=["cpu:0", "cpu:1", "cpu:2"])
with strat2.scope():
  g2 = tf.random.Generator.from_seed(1)
  cp2 = tf.train.Checkpoint(my_generator=g2)
  cp2.restore(filename)
  print("RNG stream from restoring point:")
  print(strat2.run(lambda: g2.normal([])))
  print(strat2.run(lambda: g2.normal([])))

INFO:tensorflow:Using MirroredStrategy with devices ('/job:localhost/replica:0/task:0/device:CPU:0', '/job:localhost/replica:0/task:0/device:CPU:1', '/job:localhost/replica:0/task:0/device:CPU:2')

  1: tf.Tensor(0.77539235, shape=(), dtype=float32),
  2: tf.Tensor(0.6851049, shape=(), dtype=float32)

  1: tf.Tensor(0.1251838, shape=(), dtype=float32),
  2: tf.Tensor(-0.58519536, shape=(), dtype=float32)

Although  and  are different objects from  and , they are linked via the common checkpoint file  and object name . Overlapping replicas between strategies (e.g.  and  above) will have their RNG streams properly restored like in previous examples. This guarantee doesn't cover the case when a generator is saved in a strategy scope and restored outside of any strategy scope or vice versa, because a device outside strategies is treated as different from any replica in a strategy.

 can be saved to a SavedModel. The generator can be created within a strategy scope. The saving can also happen within a strategy scope.

filename = "./saved_model"

class MyModule(tf.Module):

  def __init__(self):
    super(MyModule, self).__init__()
    self.g = tf.random.Generator.from_seed(0)

  @tf.function
  def __call__(self):
    return self.g.normal([])

  @tf.function
  def state(self):
    return self.g.state

strat = tf.distribute.MirroredStrategy(devices=["cpu:0", "cpu:1"])
with strat.scope():
  m = MyModule()
  print(strat.run(m))
  print("state:", m.state())

INFO:tensorflow:Using MirroredStrategy with devices ('/job:localhost/replica:0/task:0/device:CPU:0', '/job:localhost/replica:0/task:0/device:CPU:1')
INFO:tensorflow:Using MirroredStrategy with devices ('/job:localhost/replica:0/task:0/device:CPU:0', '/job:localhost/replica:0/task:0/device:CPU:1')
PerReplica:{
  0: tf.Tensor(-1.4154755, shape=(), dtype=float32),
  1: tf.Tensor(-0.11388441, shape=(), dtype=float32)
}
state: tf.Tensor([256   0   0], shape=(3,), dtype=int64)

with strat.scope():
  tf.saved_model.save(m, filename)
  print("RNG stream from saving point:")
  print(strat.run(m))
  print("state:", m.state())
  print(strat.run(m))
  print("state:", m.state())

INFO:tensorflow:Assets written to: ./saved_model/assets
INFO:tensorflow:Assets written to: ./saved_model/assets
RNG stream from saving point:
PerReplica:{
  0: tf.Tensor(-0.68758255, shape=(), dtype=float32),
  1: tf.Tensor(0.8084062, shape=(), dtype=float32)
}
state: tf.Tensor([512   0   0], shape=(3,), dtype=int64)
PerReplica:{
  0: tf.Tensor(-0.27342677, shape=(), dtype=float32),
  1: tf.Tensor(-0.53093255, shape=(), dtype=float32)
}
state: tf.Tensor([768   0   0], shape=(3,), dtype=int64)

imported = tf.saved_model.load(filename)
print("RNG stream from loading point:")
print("state:", imported.state())
print(imported())
print("state:", imported.state())
print(imported())
print("state:", imported.state())

RNG stream from loading point:
state: tf.Tensor([256   0   0], shape=(3,), dtype=int64)
tf.Tensor(-1.0359411, shape=(), dtype=float32)
state: tf.Tensor([512   0   0], shape=(3,), dtype=int64)
tf.Tensor(-0.06425078, shape=(), dtype=float32)
state: tf.Tensor([768   0   0], shape=(3,), dtype=int64)

Loading a SavedModel containing  into a distribution strategy is not recommended because the replicas will all generate the same random-number stream (which is because replica ID is frozen in SavedModel's graph).

Loading a distributed  (a generator created within a distribution strategy) into a non-strategy environment, like the above example, also has a caveat. The RNG state will be properly restored, but the random numbers generated will be different from the original generator in its strategy (again because a device outside strategies is treated as different from any replica in a strategy).

Usage of stateless RNGs is simple. Since they are just pure functions, there is no state or side effect involved.

print(tf.random.stateless_normal(shape=[2, 3], seed=[1, 2]))
print(tf.random.stateless_normal(shape=[2, 3], seed=[1, 2]))

tf.Tensor(
[[ 0.5441101   0.20738031  0.07356433]
 [ 0.04643455 -1.30159    -0.95385665]], shape=(2, 3), dtype=float32)
tf.Tensor(
[[ 0.5441101   0.20738031  0.07356433]
 [ 0.04643455 -1.30159    -0.95385665]], shape=(2, 3), dtype=float32)

Every stateless RNG requires a  argument, which needs to be an integer Tensor of shape . The results of the op are fully determined by this seed.

The RNG algorithm used by stateless RNGs is device-dependent, meaning the same op running on a different device may produce different outputs.

Both the  class and the  functions support the Philox algorithm (written as  or tf.random.Algorithm.PHILOX) on all devices.

Different devices will generate the same integer numbers, if using the same algorithm and starting from the same state. They will also generate "almost the same" float-point numbers, though there may be small numerical discrepancies caused by the different ways the devices carry out the float-point computation (e.g. reduction order).

On XLA-driven devices (such as TPU, and also CPU/GPU when XLA is enabled) the ThreeFry algorithm (written as  or tf.random.Algorithm.THREEFRY) is also supported. This algorithm is fast on TPU but slow on CPU/GPU compared to Philox.

items.title: Ethereum Based Meme Coin PEPETO Surpasses $5.5M In Presale
items.author: Chainwire
items.content:
Dubai, UAE, July 23rd, 2025/Chainwire/--Early-stage presales present a rare window for high upside participation. Backing a project before its official launch allows investors to enter at ground-floor valuations.

Historically, those who identified the right tokens at this stage have seen some of the most successful returns in the cryptocurrency market.

, a meme coin built on the Ethereum blockchain, has crossed $5.5 million in presale funding. With its exchange demo now live, the project is seeing increased activity as it fuses meme culture with advanced bridge and token-swap technology.

Pepeto’s Ethereum-Powered Exchange

The meme coin market is evolving, and Pepeto is at the forefront, offering more than just viral appeal. Developed on Ethereum, the project raises the bar by merging community-driven fun with real technical infrastructure.

At the heart of Pepeto lies a fully functional decentralized exchange powered by PepetoSwap technology and a seamless bridge connecting multiple chains. This means traders can avoid high-fee swaps and centralized intermediaries, enjoying fast, secure, and zero-fee transactions built on Ethereum’s trusted network.

With scalability and speed as key features, appeals to both casual users and serious investors. As Layer-2 adoption grows, it stands out as one of the few meme coins delivering tangible technical value.

Utility, Speed, and Culture: Pepeto’s Triple Edge

  • Zero-fee trading through PepetoSwap
  • Cross-chain bridge for quick, cost-effective swaps
  • Fully audited smart contract for enhanced trust
  • Viral frog narrative with strong ties to Elon and PEPE
  • Tier 1 exchange listing announcement anticipated soon

Pepeto’s Vision for the Future of Crypto

Pepeto positions itself as a transformative player in digital finance. It's Bridge exchange addresses cross-chain inefficiencies, increases liquidity, and supports smooth token transfers between networks.

A key driver of its rise is the presale, which has brought in $5.5 million so far, with tokens still priced at $0.000000142.

Its name—Power, Energy, Precision, Efficiency, Technology, and Optimization—reflects why it’s called the “God of Frogs,” representing a commitment to transparency and security for all users.

Early buyers remain confident, especially with the token yet to debut on centralized exchanges, leaving room for new investors to join before major liquidity events unfold.

Investors can take part in the $PEPETO presale now through the . Tokens are priced at $0.000000142, with ETH, USDT, and BNB accepted. Early buyers gain access to staking rewards and other exclusive ecosystem benefits.

blends the humor of meme coins with robust real-world applications. With its zero-fee exchange, cross-chain bridge, and industry-leading staking rewards, it’s setting a new standard for meme coins in 2025.
