items.title: Peter Thiel’s ‘Zero to One’ Monopoly Obsession Harms Innovation
items.author: Alex Leontaridis
items.content:
Peter Thiel’s has become a cornerstone of startup lore. A must-read manifesto promising founders a secret sauce for breakthrough innovation. Its bold premise: the path to lasting success is to build a monopoly by discovering unique “secrets” no one else knows. This idea, wrapped in Thiel’s confident, contrarian voice, has inspired countless entrepreneurs to chase blue oceans, avoid competition, and aim for market domination.

But here’s the problem: this obsession with monopoly isn’t just flawed; it can actively harm innovation. By elevating monopoly to the ultimate goal, risks encouraging founders to prioritize cornering the market over creating real value, healthy competition, and open ecosystems. Far from sparking radical new ideas, it can foster arrogance, secrecy, and a stifling environment that kills innovation’s messy, collaborative nature.

The Monopoly Obsession: A Double-Edged Sword

Thiel defines monopoly not as the evil corporate giant of old, but as a business so good at what it does that no one else can compete. While that sounds ideal, the reality is far more complicated. The fixation on monopoly can incentivize startups to erect barriers - through patents, exclusivity, or aggressive tactics - that limit competition and slow progress.

History shows that most real breakthroughs happen not in monopolies but in vibrant, competitive markets. Take the personal computing revolution: Apple, Microsoft, IBM, and countless others pushed innovation forward precisely because they competed fiercely. Monopolies often breed complacency and inefficiency. Look at companies like Kodak or Blockbuster, which dominated markets but failed to innovate and ultimately collapsed.

By promoting monopoly as the ultimate goal, risks glamorizing a dangerous endgame where the focus shifts from solving customer problems to crushing competitors. This mindset is toxic, especially for young founders hungry for quick success.

Innovation Is Often Iterative, Not “Zero to One”

The book’s framing of innovation as a singular leap ,going from zero to one, oversimplifies how breakthroughs really happen. Innovation is usually a process of iteration, learning, and evolution. Rarely does a startup create something utterly new in isolation; instead, they build on existing ideas, refine them, and compete in crowded markets.

For example, companies like Airbnb or Uber didn’t invent entirely new concepts - they innovated by improving existing services and scaling rapidly. This “one to many” progress fuels entire industries and democratizes opportunities. Thiel’s disdain for incrementalism can lead founders to dismiss valuable, practical improvements as “not innovative enough,” pushing them toward risky or unrealistic bets.

The Danger of ‘Secrets’ and Elitism

Thiel’s emphasis on “secrets” - unique insights that give startups their monopoly power - sounds inspiring but can unintentionally promote secrecy and elitism. While every startup needs some competitive advantage, elevating “secrets” to near-mystical status risks creating an exclusionary culture where founders guard information obsessively.

This secrecy can harm collaboration, knowledge sharing, and inclusivity - all essential ingredients for a thriving innovation ecosystem. It also favors those already privileged with access to networks and resources, making it harder for underrepresented founders to break through.

offers valuable lessons about the importance of vision and differentiation, but its gospel should be taken with a critical eye. Founders should balance ambition with humility, combining boldness with openness. Competition isn’t the enemy; it’s the engine that drives improvement and forces companies to truly serve customers.

The best startups don’t just seek to dominate; they seek to solve real problems in ways others can’t match. Not by crushing rivals, but by building better products and ecosystems. Innovation thrives when ideas flow freely, teams collaborate, and the market stays dynamic.

items.title: The Tech Behind War Robots’ First Sword-Wielding Mech
items.author: Dimes
items.content:
Hi! I’m Dmitry Ulitin, Senior Game Designer on War Robots project.

When we decided to add the SWORD Unit 190 robot to our game, we knew we were taking on a serious challenge. For the first time in the game's history, a robot would wield a sword — a melee weapon in a world where everyone was used to guns and long-range combat. We had already experimented with close-quarters combat using certain robot weapons, but it was never truly "melee": harpoons and AoE weapons came close, but there had never been a true “sword.” So this time, we wanted the robot to get right up in the enemy's face — and to do that, we had to build a new system.

But the new weapon wasn’t a “sword-gun” — it was a sword inside an ability. In other words, it activated after a leap and had to match the gameplay of the robot’s concept.

The concept was created in collaboration with the legendary Kunio Okawara, a designer known for creating robots for many other franchises. This time, Okawara-san designed a robot specifically for our game.

It seemed simple: a robot with a sword — a familiar idea, done many times before. But when we started implementing it, we realized there were tons of technical and creative problems we hadn’t even considered at the concept stage.

The first and most obvious problem: how do you implement melee attacks in a game where the entire combat system is built for ranged fighting?

We took the simplest approach: we used a regular gun, but made it fire within a predefined area rather than in a straight line. Technically speaking — it "shot" inside a collider. This collider shape is built from primitives, like blocks in a construction set, but invisible to the player. That let us use the game’s existing gun logic. The only difference: there's no projectile flight — the damage is dealt instantly to everyone inside the collider at the moment of attack.

\ This is similar to systems used in fighting games and slashers — we just adapted it for our needs.

\ A key detail: the collider has to be slightly bigger than the sword’s actual visual model. Otherwise, players would miss even when their hits look accurate. We enhanced this effect with a visual trail — when the sword strikes, it looks like it leaves behind an energy arc.

When the Robot Is Too Fast

So we figured out the robot would strike, but our concept also called for a raider — a robot that could fly and use its aerial position in combat. In other words, it needed to reach enemies mid-air. On paper, the concept was awesome: the robot dashes at an enemy from the sky using the game’s physics engine. It was meant to look cool and dynamic. And we thought it would.

In reality, targeting caused a big issue. The robot would accurately fly to the enemy's position — but often overshot, "flying through" them, and the hitbox was too small to land the strike once it landed. Even with the slightly larger invisible collider, it wasn’t enough. Worse still — the robot would fly through the ground because it was moving . The physics engine wasn't designed for that, especially when syncing between multiple players.

Passing through walls was rarer, so it seemed like a low-priority issue at first. We assumed there were settings that could fix the behavior — but there weren’t. We had to try other solutions.

First Iteration: Speed Boost

We gave the robot extra ground acceleration — basically, it started running faster with the sword. This helped it reach enemies and land hits even if it overshot in the air. It helped, but playing tag with enemies was still clunky.

Second Iteration: Larger Hit Zone

This almost fixed the original problem. But the more we tested, the more often the robot clipped through walls and terrain. We started to think the default physics engine just wasn’t up to the task.

Third Iteration (Most Risky): Rewrite the Physics

We created a custom layer on top of the game’s physics. The robot would now move from point A to point B using a controlled algorithm. The base physics were still there, but collision detection and pathing were handled by code. That solved everything — now the ability worked smoothly and reliably.

But How Do Robots Swing Swords?

Our animators are pros at animating machines and robots — we do it all the time. A game designer gives instructions, and animators bring the robot to life. But a robot with a sword was a different beast. After meeting with the art team, we realized the risk was higher this time and asked animators to sketch out ideas in advance.

We explored different attack styles: cross-slash, overhead, spinning slash…

\ The animations looked great — but not all of them worked for gameplay. We started importing them into the engine and testing how each swing looked from behind (since uses a third-person rear camera). No point in making an epic swing that players can't even see.

Game designers, animators, and VFX artists all met several times. This complex animation required tight collaboration between departments. We also studied other games with similar melee mechanics to see how they handled sword attacks.

Eventually, we settled on a balanced animation that looked mechanical, fit the gameplay perfectly (a side sweep), and let us fine-tune the timing.

\ Even our producer got involved — he filmed himself demonstrating different sword swings to give feedback.

When the Animation Lives Its Own Life

So we had a new animation and a new hit system — but one problem remained: the animation wasn’t synced with the sword hit. The swing would play visually, but the damage came a moment later. It looked terrible.

In , we usually build mechanics first and visuals later. Here, the ability triggered two parallel processes: the animation and the "shot." Just three seconds total, but any lag between clients caused desync between the two. This had to be fixed.

It was especially noticeable when players saw a SWORD Unit flying toward them — the swing played, but the damage came later.

We urgently reworked the animation so the swing would restart only when the "shot" triggered. And we reworked the shooting logic too: our sword was no longer just a gun firing a burst — now each attack was a single hit, with the interval controlled by the server.

This allowed us to sync the animation and hit more precisely.

To smooth the effect, we made each swing deliver three hits — just like in anime: the robot slashes once, but lands three strikes at 0.1-second intervals. That way, it looks like the sword is slicing through the target multiple times.

After all this work, we ended up with a robot that truly fights with a sword. Each challenge forced us to invent new solutions — from adapting fighting-game-style colliders to building our own impulse-dash system.

Creating the sword robot required collaboration between programmers, animators, game designers, and VFX artists. But when everything came together, it created a unique experience never before seen in .

The SWORD Unit 190 didn’t just become a new robot — it opened the door to melee combat in the game. And now that we know how to tackle these challenges, you might be seeing more sword-wielding robots in the future!

items.title: Meet TriOn & Co, Winner of Startups of The Year 2024 in Messaging & Communications
items.author: TriOn & Co
items.content:
TriOn & Co is a multi-award- winning strategic communications consultancy founded in March 2023 by Joel Lah, Marion Ang, and Charu Srivastava. We built this business on the basis that strategic communications can be a game-changer for any company or individual, regardless of industry and location. We offer tailored communications services and specialised consultancy projects crucial in today's macroeconomic climate, including business restructuring communications for organisations undergoing retrenchment or leadership changes, as well as Diversity, Equity and Inclusion (DEI) audit and strategy. TriOn operates with a regional remit and we are driven by our passion for doing good work while creating a safe and respectful work environment that not only benefits our team and ourselves but the impact can be seen in the work that we do for our clients.

Tell us how your startup is changing the world.

We're transforming the communications industry by proving that exceptional client results and employee well-being aren't mutually exclusive—they're synergistic. Traditional agencies are notorious for its fast-paced and high pressure environment, causing talent burnout for short-term gains. We're demonstrating that a sustainable model with people-first practices can actually drive efficiency and better business outcomes. Our full suite of communications services across financial services, professional services, government, and enterprise technology sectors showcases this philosophy in action. Our advocacy for diversity and inclusion, mental health and workplace wellness, and active mentoring of industry talent creates a ripple effect, raising standards across the communications sector.

We're distinguished by our three co-founders - Joel Lah, Marion Ang, and Charu Srivastava - who function like pieces of a jigsaw puzzle, combining complementary strengths while sharing deep chemistry and values from their successful individual careers including being ex-colleagues. We have an 8-point manifesto that is our operational blueprint that prioritises relationships over money, maintains transparency through public accountability, and creates a workplace where "TriOnings" thrive under progressive policies like our 4.5-day work week, flexible work arrangements, unlimited leave, and strict anti-burnout measures. This values-driven approach has delivered exceptional results: 100% retainer client retention and a new business conversion rate that jumped from 26% to 44%—all achieved through referrals, never cold outreach. Our strong reach through networks and associations, combined with human-centric culture and proven track record, demonstrates that our business can achieve success and results without compromising on employee-driven values.

What does it mean for you to win this title?

Firstly a big thank you to the mystery person who believed in us enough to nominate TriOn & Co - this means the world to us and validates the work that we do. The award came through open voting, which means our clients, peers and industry partners actively voted for us and we’re truly very proud to have made a positive impact on people. TriOn & Co is only 2 years old and this award win proves that we’re on the right path. The win inspires us to work harder and continue pushing boundaries to work smarter and better.

What do you love about your team, and why are you the ones to set out for this mission?

The founders share the same core values and have unshakeable trust in each other's ability to not just generate great ideas, but execute them flawlessly. Each of us has a "get shit done" mindset and strong chemistry that makes critical business decisions seamless. Additionally our shared sense of humour keeps us grounded through challenges. We foster innovation by being explicitly supportive of the use of technology in our work, encouraging our team to leverage tools smartly and ethically to enhance their output. When you combine proven execution capabilities with aligned values and a culture that embraces intelligent innovation, you create the perfect foundation for delivering consistently high standards of meaningful output, and perhaps set the wheels in motion to change the industry.

Looking back, what milestone was the biggest turning point for TriOn & Co?

It’s just the beginning for us, so our “biggest” turning point is yet to come. However, one memorable milestone in our journey so far was when we were able to pay ourselves as founders. We’re bootstrapped and made the intentional decision to not pay ourselves any salary until we had reached our first financial target. From the very beginning we were clear about prioritising investment in people and building the foundational processes and services to set a strong base for future growth. We actually hit this milestone in just six months, which is something we’re immensely proud of.

What’s one valuable lesson you learned that you’d pass on to other startups?

Don’t let the upfront costs of investment in proper processes and policies deter you. For us to grow effectively as a company that prioritises its people, we need to ensure our people processes and policies are well-established before we expand. We also invested in engaging a lawyer to ensure the proper legal set-up of the company before we even launched. The people and the culture you foster will make or break the company. My advice to other startups: don’t wait till you’re bigger to invest in these processes - build them while you’re still agile enough to get them right.

AI will continue to be a disrupting force but we see AI as an enabler that makes us more productive rather than a threat to what we do. Communications is more important today than ever before as businesses realise the value of communications to connect with audiences in today's digital age. Engaging with audiences beyond digital communications channels requires critical thinking, emotional intelligence like empathy and authenticity that are the basis of mutual understanding, building connections and forming relationships AI can try to mimic this but it does not have the ability to manage or understand emotions, a critical aspect of communications, which can come only from our human intelligence, that is a blend of thinking and emotional intelligence

How does TriOn & Co intend to embrace the responsibility of this title in 2025?

We want to highlight the value of communications in today’s highly polarised world. Our focus is on leveraging the power of communications that can shape perceptions, bring to light challenges, showcase positive impact and raise awareness on issues of matter. At the same time, there is a huge responsibility to do this authentically, as audiences increasingly feel misled or ‘scammed’ in a world shaped by AI. That’s why we’re here to champion human-centered communication that is credible and trustworthy.

We have achieved so much in our first two years and won multiple awards that validates our efforts, including the recognition from Hackernoon. However, this is just the beginning for TriOn and we are looking for a long journey of sustainable growth. We want to build on this momentum and grow as a company, business and team. The ultimate goal is to create outstanding value for our clients, produce great quality work and people.

2024 has been crazy, with all the new techs, and all the geopolitics fluctuations. What are the impacts of these to your startup, and to your industry as a whole? Be as brief or as detailed as you like.

2024 and 2025 to-date have further emphasised the role of communications, and the importance of our industry in shaping perceptions and building trust in people and brands. This has become even more crucial because we’re living in a world of increased fake news, misinformation and.

We would love your feedback on HackerNoon as a tech publication! How has your experience been with us?

It’s been a great experience and we’re happy to have been inducted into Hacknoon’s Startups of the Year hall of fame! Your platform has provided increased visibility for us and helped us connect with a highly engaged tech community.

Any words of wisdom you’d like to share with us?

The startup journey isn’t for everyone, but it is an incredible experience when you’re pursuing it for the right reasons. Having strong morals and values is essential, and there is no substitute for clear communication and hard work. Invest in a strong foundation, including proper legal structures and internal policies from the start.

items.title: Court Battles Spark an Unexpected AI Movement: Fairness by Design
items.content:
The courtroom battles over AI discrimination have sparked something unexpected: a renaissance in fairness engineering. While legal scholars debate liability frameworks and regulators draft compliance guidelines, a parallel revolution is unfolding in the technical community. Engineers, data scientists, and algorithmic auditors are developing sophisticated tools and methodologies that don't just identify bias—they prevent it, measure it, and actively correct it in real-time.

This shift represents more than incremental progress. It's a fundamental reimagining of how we build AI systems, where fairness becomes a core engineering requirement rather than an afterthought. The stakes couldn't be higher: as AI systems make increasingly consequential decisions about our lives, the technical infrastructure for ensuring fairness may determine whether these technologies become instruments of justice or perpetuate historical inequities.

Traditional approaches to AI bias have been largely reactive—organizations deploy systems, discover discriminatory outcomes through legal challenges or public outcry, then scramble to fix them. But a new generation of technical solutions is flipping this paradigm. Instead of waiting for discrimination to surface, these tools bake fairness directly into the machine learning pipeline.

Consider the evolution beyond Amazon's infamous recruiting debacle. While that case highlighted how historical hiring data could perpetuate gender bias, it also catalyzed development of sophisticated bias detection frameworks. Modern hiring algorithms now employ techniques like adversarial debiasing, where neural networks are trained not just to make accurate predictions, but to make predictions that cannot be easily distinguished by a secondary "discriminator" network designed to detect protected characteristics.

"We're moving from 'bias in, bias out' to 'bias in, fairness out,'" explains Dr. Sarah Chen, a principal research scientist at a major tech company. "The algorithms themselves are becoming active participants in promoting equity."

The Mathematics of Justice: Quantifying Fairness

One of the most significant technical advances has been the development of rigorous mathematical frameworks for measuring fairness. Where legal standards often rely on broad concepts like "disparate impact," engineers now work with precise mathematical definitions that can be implemented in code.

Equalized odds, demographic parity, individual fairness, counterfactual fairness—these aren't just academic concepts anymore. They're becoming standard metrics that engineers optimize for, similar to how they might optimize for accuracy or computational efficiency. This mathematization of fairness has profound implications for how courts evaluate AI systems, providing objective benchmarks that can be independently verified.

The COMPAS criminal risk assessment tool, central to the landmark State v. Loomis case, exemplifies both the challenges and opportunities in this space. While the Wisconsin Supreme Court upheld COMPAS despite concerns about algorithmic opacity, the controversy spurred development of open-source alternatives that are both more accurate and more fair. These new systems employ techniques like fairness-constrained optimization and multi-objective learning, simultaneously optimizing for predictive accuracy and equitable outcomes across racial groups.

Explainability as a Technical Discipline

The legal demand for algorithmic transparency, crystallized in cases like Loomis and reinforced by GDPR requirements, has transformed explainable AI from a nice-to-have feature into a core technical requirement. This has driven remarkable innovation in interpretability techniques.

Modern explainability tools go far beyond simple feature importance scores. Techniques like LIME (Local Interpretable Model-agnostic Explanations), SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations), and counterfactual explanations can now provide detailed, individualized explanations for AI decisions. More importantly, these tools can identify when explanations might reveal or perpetuate bias.

For instance, in credit scoring applications like the Apple Card controversy, advanced explainability techniques can detect when seemingly neutral features (like zip code or shopping patterns) serve as proxies for protected characteristics. This technical capability transforms legal compliance from a post-hoc audit exercise into a real-time monitoring system.

Privacy-Preserving Fairness: The Next Frontier

Perhaps the most exciting technical development is the emergence of privacy-preserving fairness techniques. Traditional bias testing often requires access to sensitive demographic information, creating a tension between fairness and privacy. But breakthrough techniques in differential privacy, federated learning, and secure multi-party computation are resolving this apparent contradiction.

These methods allow organizations to detect and correct bias without centralizing sensitive personal data. For example, federated fairness algorithms can identify discriminatory patterns across multiple institutions' datasets without any single entity having access to the complete information. This is particularly valuable in healthcare AI, where privacy regulations often conflict with the data access needed for bias auditing.

Algorithmic Auditing: From Art to Science

The technical maturation of algorithmic auditing represents another paradigm shift. Early bias assessments were often ad-hoc exercises conducted by individual researchers or advocacy groups. Today, algorithmic auditing is becoming a professional discipline with standardized methodologies, certification programs, and specialized tooling.

Automated auditing platforms can now continuously monitor AI systems for fairness violations, similar to how cybersecurity tools monitor for threats. These systems can detect subtle forms of bias that might escape human reviewers, such as intersectional discrimination affecting multiple protected groups simultaneously.

The healthcare algorithm study published in Science, which revealed systematic underestimation of Black patients' health risks, exemplifies this evolution. While that discovery relied on academic research, similar patterns would now be caught by routine algorithmic auditing processes deployed in healthcare systems.

The convergence of legal requirements and technical capabilities is giving birth to "regulatory technology" or "regtech" specifically for AI fairness. These platforms translate complex legal standards into executable code, making compliance both more reliable and more cost-effective.

For instance, the EU's proposed AI Act includes specific technical requirements for high-risk AI systems. Rather than leaving implementation to individual interpretation, regtech platforms provide standardized implementations of these requirements, complete with automated testing and reporting capabilities.

This technical standardization has profound implications for global AI governance. When legal standards can be implemented in code, compliance becomes portable across jurisdictions and verifiable by automated systems.

The Democratization of Fairness Engineering

Open-source tools are democratizing access to fairness engineering capabilities. Projects like AIF360 (AI Fairness 360), Fairlearn, and What-If Tool provide sophisticated bias detection and mitigation capabilities to any organization, regardless of their technical resources.

This democratization is crucial for addressing the "fairness divide"—the risk that only large technology companies can afford to build fair AI systems. By providing accessible tools and standardized methodologies, the open-source community is ensuring that fairness engineering capabilities can scale across the entire AI ecosystem.

Real-Time Fairness: The Streaming Revolution

Traditional bias testing has been a batch process—algorithms are trained, tested for bias, and then deployed. But modern AI systems often learn continuously from streaming data, which can introduce bias dynamically. This has driven development of real-time fairness monitoring systems.

These systems can detect fairness violations as they occur and automatically trigger corrective actions. For example, if an online advertising system begins showing discriminatory patterns in real-time, fairness monitoring tools can immediately adjust the algorithm's parameters to restore equity.

This capability is particularly important in dynamic environments where data distributions change over time. A hiring algorithm that was fair when trained might become biased as the applicant pool changes, but real-time monitoring can catch and correct these drifts automatically.

The Human-AI Collaboration Model

Despite remarkable technical advances, the most effective fairness engineering approaches recognize that algorithmic solutions must be paired with human oversight. This has led to sophisticated human-AI collaboration models where algorithms handle routine bias detection and correction while escalating complex cases to human reviewers.

These hybrid systems can process the massive scale of modern AI deployments while maintaining the nuanced judgment needed for complex fairness determinations. They represent a practical middle ground between fully automated systems and pure human review.

Economic Incentives for Fairness

Technical advances are also addressing the economic barriers to fairness. Early bias mitigation techniques often required significant computational overhead and accuracy trade-offs, making them expensive to deploy. But newer approaches like efficient neural architecture search for fairness and lightweight bias correction techniques are making fairness engineering cost-effective.

Some advanced techniques even demonstrate that fairer algorithms can be more accurate in many scenarios, particularly when training data is limited or noisy. This alignment of fairness and performance incentives is crucial for widespread adoption.

International Standards and Technical Harmonization

The development of international technical standards for AI fairness is accelerating global convergence in how these systems are built and evaluated. Standards bodies like IEEE, ISO, and W3C are developing detailed technical specifications that transcend national legal frameworks.

These standards provide a common technical language for fairness that enables international cooperation and mutual recognition of compliance efforts. They're particularly important for multinational technology companies that must navigate diverse legal requirements across jurisdictions.

Looking Forward: The Infrastructure of Algorithmic Justice

The technical revolution in fairness engineering is creating the infrastructure for algorithmic justice. Just as we have technical standards for security, reliability, and performance, we're developing comprehensive technical standards for fairness and non-discrimination.

This infrastructure includes not just the algorithms themselves, but the entire ecosystem of tools, methodologies, standards, and professional practices needed to build fair AI systems at scale. It represents a fundamental shift in how we approach AI development—from ad-hoc bias testing to systematic fairness engineering.

The legal cases that initially exposed AI discrimination problems have thus catalyzed something much larger: a technical transformation that promises to make fairness a core characteristic of AI systems. As this infrastructure matures, the question is no longer whether we can build fair AI systems, but whether we have the collective will to deploy them.

The engineers building tomorrow's AI systems have unprecedented tools for promoting justice and equity. The legal framework provides the mandate, but the technical community is providing the means. Together, they're writing the code for a more equitable future.
